text.title: mvp-scoping Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for mvp-scoping. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Start with MVP to validate the core hypothesis, then evolve to MLP for retention. Do not skip validation to go straight to polish.
rule.rationale: Polishing a product before validating demand is expensive. MVP first confirms the problem-solution fit exists; MLP then makes the solution worth keeping.

rule.statement: Categorise every feature as Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, or Won't Have. Must-Have count must not exceed 5. Total effort for Must+Should must fit within the target timebox.
rule.rationale: Without a hard cap on Must-Haves, scope creep makes MVPs into 6-month projects. The cap forces real trade-off decisions rather than deferring them to "later".

rule.statement: Every MVP scope document must include a kill signal: the specific evidence that would prove the hypothesis wrong and stop the project. Example: "If fewer than 10% of users complete the core flow within 2 weeks, shut down."
rule.rationale: Without a kill signal, an MVP becomes an unfalsifiable experiment. Teams will ship regardless of what they learn, because there is no pre-agreed failure criterion to act on.

rule.statement: Write the problem statement using this template: "For [target user] who [has this problem], our MVP will prove that [hypothesis] by delivering [core outcome] in [timeframe] with [constraints]."
rule.rationale: The template forces explicit hypothesis statement before scoping begins. Scope without a hypothesis produces features without a learning goal.

rule.statement: Answer: "What is the ONE thing this product must do well?" Apply the value test: if you remove this feature, is the product useless? If yes, it is core value. If no, it is not.
rule.rationale: Every scope decision derives from the core value. Features that do not support or enable the core value are candidates for Won't Have regardless of how good they are on their own.

rule.statement: Brain-dump all features first, then categorise each as Core (product doesn't work without it), Important (users would miss it), Nice-to-have (users can wait), or Out of scope.
rule.rationale: Categorising before applying MoSCoW prevents anchoring on the first few ideas. The full dump also creates the Won't Have list, which is as important as the Must Have list.

rule.statement: Assign each feature a MoSCoW priority. Verify: Must-Haves do not exceed 5, total Must+Should effort fits the timebox. If either check fails, cut Must-Haves before extending the timeline.
rule.rationale: Extending the timeline to accommodate scope is the default failure mode. The constraint forces real prioritisation instead of deferring trade-offs to engineering.

rule.statement: Run five checks before locking scope: solves core problem end-to-end, first user can complete core job without future features, value delivered is testable, buildable in target timeframe, has a testable hypothesis.
rule.rationale: Scope that fails any check will fail in production. End-to-end validation is the most commonly skipped check — partial flows deliver no value even when they function correctly.

rule.statement: Write explicit completion criteria: feature list with acceptance criteria, definition of "good enough" quality, what to measure or learn, and the next decision after MVP results arrive (validate → build, invalidate → pivot).
rule.rationale: MVP without a definition of done runs indefinitely. The next-decision field closes the loop: teams must decide in advance what they will do with the results.
